title: The third drawer
summary: A grandfather kept three drawers in his desk. The third was always closed. After he died, his family opened it.
body:
My grandfather had three drawers in his desk. The first drawer had pens and old keys. The second drawer had photos of his family. The third drawer was always closed.

After he died, my mother and I opened the third drawer. Inside, we found maps. There were maps of Paris, Rome, Tokyo, and many other cities. Some maps were old. Some were new. All of them had small notes in his handwriting.

My grandfather never went to any of these places. He worked at the same factory for fifty years. He took the bus to work. He came home for dinner at six. He did not have money for trips.

But every Sunday afternoon, he sat at his desk. He opened the third drawer. He read the maps and made small notes. 'Eiffel Tower — Monday morning, fewer people.' 'Trastevere — best café on the corner.'

He visited these cities in his mind. He planned every day. He never went, but he was not unhappy. He just wanted to know the streets, the cafés, the small parks.

My mother and I looked at each other. We did not know what to say. The third drawer was full of trips he never took. It was also full of joy.

grammarNotes.title: Past simple (regular and irregular)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng quá khứ đơn để kể chuyện đã xảy ra. Động từ thường thêm -ed (worked, opened), nhưng nhiều động từ bất quy tắc (had, went, took, came, found, sat). Trong bài: 'He worked at the same factory.' 'He took the bus to work.' 'We found maps.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The story uses past simple to describe what the grandfather did. Regular verbs add -ed ('worked', 'opened'). Irregular verbs change form ('had', 'went', 'took', 'came', 'found', 'sat'). Example: 'He took the bus to work.'

translationVi:
Ông tôi có ba ngăn kéo trong bàn làm việc. Ngăn thứ nhất có bút và chìa khóa cũ. Ngăn thứ hai có ảnh gia đình. Ngăn thứ ba luôn đóng.

Sau khi ông mất, mẹ và tôi mở ngăn thứ ba. Bên trong, chúng tôi thấy nhiều bản đồ. Có bản đồ Paris, Rome, Tokyo, và nhiều thành phố khác. Một số bản đồ cũ. Một số mới. Tất cả đều có ghi chú nhỏ bằng chữ viết tay của ông.

Ông tôi chưa bao giờ đến những nơi này. Ông làm việc ở cùng một nhà máy suốt năm mươi năm. Ông đi xe buýt đi làm. Ông về nhà ăn tối lúc sáu giờ. Ông không có tiền cho các chuyến đi.

Nhưng chiều Chủ nhật nào ông cũng ngồi vào bàn. Ông mở ngăn thứ ba. Ông đọc các bản đồ và ghi chú nhỏ. 'Tháp Eiffel — sáng thứ Hai, ít người hơn.' 'Trastevere — quán cà phê đẹp nhất ở góc phố.'

Ông đi thăm các thành phố ấy trong đầu. Ông lên kế hoạch từng ngày. Ông chưa bao giờ đi, nhưng ông không buồn. Ông chỉ muốn biết các con đường, các quán cà phê, các công viên nhỏ.

Mẹ và tôi nhìn nhau. Chúng tôi không biết nói gì. Ngăn thứ ba đầy những chuyến đi ông chưa bao giờ thực hiện. Nó cũng đầy niềm vui.

questions.prompt: How many drawers did the grandfather have in his desk?
questions.explanation: 'My grandfather had three drawers in his desk.'

questions.prompt: What was in the first drawer?
questions.explanation: 'The first drawer had pens and old keys.'

questions.prompt: What was in the third drawer?

questions.explanation: 'He worked at the same factory for fifty years.'

questions.prompt: How did he travel to work?
When did the grandfather sit at his desk?
questions.explanation: 'But every Sunday afternoon, he sat at his desk.'

questions.prompt: Did the grandfather ever go to Paris, Rome, or Tokyo?
questions.explanation: 'My grandfather never went to any of these places.'

questions.prompt: Why did the grandfather make notes on the maps?
options: He visited the cities in his mind.
He was learning a new language.
questions.explanation: 'He visited these cities in his mind. He planned every day.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer feel about what they found in the third drawer?
options: Surprised and moved — full of joy as well as unmade trips
Angry that the grandfather did not travel
Confused about who owned the maps
questions.explanation: 'The third drawer was full of trips he never took. It was also full of joy.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the story?
options: A small private life can still hold large dreams.
Travel is the only way to be happy.
Maps are better than real cities.
questions.explanation: The grandfather never travelled, but the maps and notes show a full inner life of imagined trips.
questions.hint: Think about why the writer says the drawer was 'full of joy.'

blanks.explanation: He had three drawers in his desk.
The second drawer had photos of his family.
Inside the third drawer they found maps.
He never went to any of these places.
He worked at the same factory.
He did not have money for trips.
Every Sunday afternoon he sat at his desk.
He visited these cities in his mind.
The drawer was also full of joy.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The grandfather travelled to many cities only in his mind. Is a place you have imagined for a long time, but never visited, in some way still part of your life? What do we keep when we keep a plan we never act on?
